coredata.dc:title: Conjugate of graphene quantum dots and glutaminase for the sensing of L-glutamine: Electrochemical vs. fluorescent sensing approaches
coredata.dc:creator: Devi, Pooja
Kukkar, Deepak
Kaur, Manpreet
Thakur, Anupma
Kim, Ki-Hyun
Kukkar, Preeti
Kaur, Kamalpreet
Kaur, Harsimran
coredata.dc:description: This research describes the synthesis of a covalent bioconjugate of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and glutaminase (10 units·mg−1) for electrochemical and photoluminescent (PL) detection of L-glutamine (Gln). As synthesized GQDs exhibited uniform size distribution, (average diameter ~5–10 nm, and discrete blue emission characteristics (λex = 391 ± 2 nm and λem = 480 ± 2 nm)). Accordingly, the immobilization of glutaminase over the surface of the GQDs was confirmed by the characteristic Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy peaks of the amide bond at 1,703 and 1,390 cm−1. The conjugate was used to detect Gln using electrochemical and PL approaches on a parallel basis over a concentration range of 100–1,000 µM. Electrochemical analysis of Gln using cyclic voltammetry revealed a limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) values of 360.4 µM and 1,100 µM, respectively. In comparison, quantification of Gln based on the PL approach yielded a Stern-Volmer quenching constant, LOD, and LOQ values of 2.2 × 103 M−1, 9.42 µM, and 28.57 µM, respectively. As such, the PL spectroscopy-based analysis was seen to have better sensitivity for Gln quantification than the electrochemical analysis. The PL-based approach is a more recommendable option for Gln quantification with high sensitivity and rapidity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cyclic voltammetry
Glutamine
Glutaminase
Graphene quantum dots
Sensing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of GQDs (A) and GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate. HRTEM images of GQDs (B and C) and the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate (D, E and F). Scale bar in A, B, C, and D correspond to 100 nm and in E and F corresponds to 50 and 5 nm, respectively.

Crystallographic and PL studies of the GQDs showing: (A) XRD spectra of GO (orange curve) and GQDs (blue curve); PL excitation (blue curve: (λmax)ex = 392 ± 2 nm) and emission spectra (orange curve: (λmax)em = 480 ± 2 nm)) of GQDs showing a characteristic blue emission profile. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Variations in CV characteristics (10 mM PBS (pH = 7.0)/-0.5 to 0.5 V/N2 atmosphere/50 mV·sec−1) of GCE: (a) bare electrode; (b) 10 mM Gln reacted GCE; (c) GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate reacted electrode; and (d) GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate catalyzed Gln oxidation at different Gln concentrations (100, 294, 481, 660, and 917 µM).

The basic analytical performance of the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate based on the CV method in Fe2+/Fe3+ redox system. (A) Redox behaviour of GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate as a function of applied potential (−0.5 to 0.5 V) over various scanning rates (10–100 mV·s−1). (B) Relation in anodic and cathodic peak currents with square root of scan rate. (C) Areal capacitance vs. scan rate, (D) calibration curve showing current (I) vs Gln concentration.

PL studies showing: (A) Fluorescence response of the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate over a Gln concentration range of 100–1,000 µM. The blank curve refers to the pristine GQDs, (B) Relative linear decrease in the PL intensity (I/I0) of GQD-glutaminase bioconjugate as a function of Gln concentration (200–900 µM).
CV analysis showing Ip values over various concentrations of Gln added to GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate along with chitosan scanned over 0 and 0.4 V.

row.entry: Concentrations of GQDs - glutaminase bioconjugate along with chitosan (µM)
Volume of Gln in DI water (µl)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Performance comparison of GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate with similar nanoprobes for biosensing applications.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: BpsA = Blue pigment synthase A; GlnBP = Glutamine binding protein; GQDs = Graphene quantum dots; PBS = Phosphate buffer saline.

head.ce:title: Conjugate of graphene quantum dots and glutaminase for the sensing of L-glutamine: Electrochemical vs. fluorescent sensing approaches

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally as first author.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research describes the synthesis of a covalent bioconjugate of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and glutaminase (10 units·mg−1) for electrochemical and photoluminescent (PL) detection of L-glutamine (Gln). As synthesized GQDs exhibited uniform size distribution, (average diameter ~5–10 nm, and discrete blue emission characteristics (λex = 391 ± 2 nm and λem = 480 ± 2 nm)). Accordingly, the immobilization of glutaminase over the surface of the GQDs was confirmed by the characteristic Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy peaks of the amide bond at 1,703 and 1,390 cm−1. The conjugate was used to detect Gln using electrochemical and PL approaches on a parallel basis over a concentration range of 100–1,000 µM. Electrochemical analysis of Gln using cyclic voltammetry revealed a limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) values of 360.4 µM and 1,100 µM, respectively. In comparison, quantification of Gln based on the PL approach yielded a Stern-Volmer quenching constant, LOD, and LOQ values of 2.2 × 103 M−1, 9.42 µM, and 28.57 µM, respectively. As such, the PL spectroscopy-based analysis was seen to have better sensitivity for Gln quantification than the electrochemical analysis. The PL-based approach is a more recommendable option for Gln quantification with high sensitivity and rapidity.

ce:sections.ce:para: Glutamine (Gln) is one of the most abundant amino acids in the human body. It is the foremost energy source for carrying out various physiological functions [1]. The major functions of Gln include: (a) regulation of the redox condition of cells, (b) metabolic intermediate, (c) neurotransmitter in the central nervous system, and (d) nitrogen transport in cells [2,3]. The normal concentration ranges of Gln in blood plasma and the interstitial fluid are generally observed to be 0.6 and 2–20 mM, respectively [4]. The progression of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is suggested to be an effect of the alteration of Gln metabolism [5]. Within the mitochondria, the catalytic enzyme L-glutaminase causes the hydrolytic deamination of Gln, producing glutamic acid and ammonia [6]. It is a key enzyme for the conversion of Gln into glutamate and is expressed in response to DNA damage and oxidative stress. This favors the rapid production of glutamate for its use in the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle. Elevation in the expression of this enzyme facilitates Gln metabolism, thereby decreasing the production of reactive oxygen species. As a result, the oxidation of DNA in normal and stressed cells decreases appreciably [7]. The product glutamate metabolizes further into α-ketoglutarate to enter the TCA for the generation of adenosine triphosphate in normal as well as cancerous cells [8]. However, in tumor cells, the higher expression level of glutaminase has been correlated with poor prognosis [9]. Another, p53 protein-dependent, phosphate-activated glutaminase is known to have an antioxidant defense mechanism [10]. Cancer cells can undergo apoptosis mediated by MYC, a proto-oncogene that undergoes downstream signal transduction pathways in response to growth factors, under conditions of Gln depletion, which explains the catalytic role of L-glutaminase in the conversion of Gln to glutamate [11]. In view of this discussion, L-glutaminase has been suggested as a biomarker to determine variation in Gln levels [12]. Accordingly, L-glutaminase-catalyzed conversion of Gln to glutamate may serve as an important biomarker for tumor detection in both in vitro and in vivo conditions.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nanometer-sized planar graphene sheets that exhibit size-tunable PL characteristics, acceptable biocompatibility, and high surface reactivity [13–15]. Compared to other reported graphene structures, GQDs exhibit strong quantum confinement and edge effects owing to their miniaturized dimensions [16–18]. These characteristics confer GQDs with the potential to be a fluorescent (FL) probe with high specificity and selectivity [19–21]. Moreover, their tunable functionalization with a range of different functional groups enhances their ability to combine with diverse bioactive components, such as enzymes and anti-cancer drugs, through covalent bond formation [22]. Through the formation of such bioconjugates, GQDs can expand their applicability toward the detection of various target analytes including cancer biomarkers in solution form [23,24].

The miniaturized dimension of the GQDs enabled the expansion of their contact area with the analyte, which is especially important when targeting electrochemically active analytes [25]. Consequently, the immobilization of GQDs on some substrates (e.g., glass and carbon) can be used as an effective option to enhanceelectrochemical reactions [26]. Moreover, the abundant chemical functionalities and the semiconductor properties provide GQDs with the opportunities for enzyme-based fluorescence and electrochemical detection because of their inherent fluorescence characteristics and facile bioconjugation capability [27]. In light of these observations, enormous efforts have been made to develop GQDs-based electrochemical and fluorescence-based sensing of various analytes including pesticides [28], amino acids [26], disease-specific biomarkers [29,30], neurotransmitters [31], metal ions [32], and phyto-alkaloids [33,34].

Evidence collected to date suggests that GQDs-based luminescence is a method of choice for biosensing applications [35–37]. To the best of our knowledge, GQDs-based sensing of Gln has rarely been performed with respect to electrochemical or PL approaches. To this end, the feasibility of the as-prepared GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate was tested for the detection of Gln through cyclic voltammetry (CV) and FL methods. A detailed description of various synthesis, characterization, and sensing protocols is provided in the supplementary information (SI). In the CV approach, the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate was used to measure changes in the cathodic and anodic current as a function of Gln concentration. For a comparative study, the PL approach was also used to check the variation in luminescence characteristics of the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate in the presence of Gln at various concentrations. This approach offered a lower LOD for Gln quantification compared to the CV-based approach. Overall, our approach is less time consuming to achieve highly sensitive detection of Gln relative to the other options available for Gln biosensing.

GQDs with a high PL intensity were synthesized by a simple acid reflux method from previously prepared GO. Fig. S4 shows the UV–visible spectra of GQDs and the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate dispersed in aqueous medium. GQDs with a characteristic peak at 333 nm should show the n-π* transition of the CO band, which is in line with the previous reports [38,39]. However, the UV–visible spectrum of the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate had a sharp peak (365 nm), corresponding to the n-π* transition of the amide CN bond. The chemical composition of the GQDs and GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate was characterized with FTIR spectroscopy, as presented in Fig. S5. The FTIR spectrum of the GQDs contained a sharp peak at 1094 cm−1 caused by the CO stretching. A broad absorption peak at 3,451 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of OH groups. In addition, the carboxyl CO group stretching was observed as a feeble peak at 1,640 cm−1. Likewise, the FTIR spectrum of glutaminase showed a sharp band at 1,096 cm−1 caused by the CO stretching vibrations. Additionally, the bands at 1,525 and 1,453 cm−1 could be assigned to CC stretching vibrations of the aromatic side chains. The bands at 3,423 and 3,734 cm−1 (and beyond) were ascribed to the NH and aqueous OH vibrations in the glutaminase molecule, respectively. Finally, the FTIR spectrum of the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate showed a distinct band at 1,703 cm−1, which indicated the formation of the amide CO bond between the GQDs and glutaminase. Moreover, the bands at 2923 and 1569 cm−1 reflected the CH stretching and carboxylate CO stretching, respectively [40,41]. The distinct band at 1390 cm−1 signified the presence of OH bending vibrations due to the formation of the covalent amide linkage.

The morphological characterization of the GQDs and GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate was further carried out with TEM and HRTEM (Fig. 3). The TEM and HRTEM images of GQDs (average diameter ~5–10 nm) revealed their agglomeration in aqueous medium (Fig. 1 A and B). Nonetheless, GQDs exhibited a highly crystalline structure, as shown by the distinct in-line lattice spacing observed in the magnified view in Fig. 1C. However, upon conjugation with glutaminase, their surface was decorated by the enzyme. Consequently, highly agglomerated clusters were observed in the TEM image presented in Fig. 1D. Consequently, a unique lattice spacing was not observed for the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate, as shown in Fig. 1E and F. Overall, the electron microscopy analysis confirmed the formation of the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate, matching the spectroscopic observations.

The variation in the crystalline nature of the GO and GQDs was compared by XRD analysis as shown in Fig. 2
A. The XRD pattern of the GO was characterized by a sharp peak at 10.96° (orange curve, Fig. 2A). The appearance of a sharp peak suggested complete oxidation of the graphitic carbon in excess KMnO4 as reported elsewhere. Further, this peak is ascribable to the sp2 and sp3 domains from pristine graphite and oxidized graphite, respectively [42,43]. However, the XRD pattern of GQDs (blue curve, Fig. 2A) was characterized by the presence of a broad peak at around 25.43°, which corresponds to the (002) peak in these nanocrystals. This peak was in accordance with previous reports (JCPDS Card No. 75-0444) to confirm the formation of crystalline GQDs [44,45]. In addition to this prominent peak, a relative less intense peak at 43.68° was also present in the XRD spectrum of GQDs, which could be assigned to the presence of some impurities during the synthesis process. To explore the PL characteristics of GQDs, PL excitation and emission spectrum were determined, as shown in Fig. 2B. GQDs exhibited a relatively broad excitation peak (blue curve: (λmax)ex = 392 ± 2 nm), which was in agreement with their UV–visible absorption spectrum [16]. However, their emission profile rendered a distinct blue emission peak (orange curve: (λmax)em = 480 ± 2 nm) because of their ultra-fine dimensions [38]. Moreover, the emission profile of the GQDs remained consistent for more than 6 months to highlight their good stability.

GQDs functionalized with glutaminase enzyme were employed for the fabrication of a biosensor based on electrochemical analysis for the detection of Gln. The CV-assisted sensing of Gln was conducted by monitoring its oxidation to glutamic acid in the presence of the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate on a GCE surface. To this end, the electrochemical performance of bare GCE and Gln-treated GCE were compared over a −0.5 to 0.5 V potential range. Fig. 3A, B show the CV of the bare GCE in PBS (pH = 7.0) in the absence and presence of Gln (10 mM), respectively, with the potential ranging from −0.5 to 0.5 V. Further, electrochemical analysis of Gln was carried out with the GCE modified with the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate. Fig. 3C shows the CV analysis of the GCE modified with the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate, as described in Section 2.2.3. The GCE modified with the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate showed one cathodic and one anodic peak at −0.1 and 0.3 V, respectively. As shown in previous research, Gln alone is electrochemically inactive over this potential range [46]. Next, a CV analysis was conducted using samples of Gln prepared at various concentrations (100 μM to 1 mM, Table 1 ). After the addition of Gln at various concentrations, changes in the anodic and cathodic peak currents were observed, as shown in Fig. 3D. The increase in the anodic and cathodic peak currents was seen consistently across varying concentrations of Gln (100–917 µM) when tested relative to blank samples. This observed pattern can be ascribed to variation in the oxidation state of Gln to yield glutamic acid in the presence of the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate (Table S1).

To learn more about the variation in cathodic and anodic peak currents, the impedance characteristics of Gln oxidation catalyzed by GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate were evaluated across varying concentrations of Gln. EIS measurements were recorded over a frequency range of 0.1 to 3 × 105 Hz, as shown in Fig. S6. The EIS response indicated that the polarization impedance for 917 µM of Gln was very low compared to that for 100 µM of Gln. These observations suggested that the oxidation of Gln to glutamic acid catalysed by the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugation was substantially dependent upon the oxidation state of Gln. This was clearly inferred by means of the observed reduced impedance, which could play a catalytic role in the electrochemical detection of Gln.

The effect of various scanning rates on the activity of GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate was also investigated with increasing scan rates (10–100 mV·s−1), as presented in Fig. 4 A. The increase in scan rate caused an increase in the peak current with an anodic shift (i a) of the oxidation peak and a cathodic shift (i c) of the reduction peak. The linear relation between ia and ic with square root of scan rate, confirm that the reaction is diffusion controlled. Further, the ratio of i a/i c, was observed to be 0.75, which indicated the irreversible redox reaction, attributed to limited transport of redox species, Fe2+/Fe3+, at GQDs-glutaminase modified electrode interface. Thereafter, the areal capacitance (C) was calculated to analyze the dependence of electron transfer kinetics on scan rate. The value of C was discerned to decrease from 9.25 × 10−5 to 2.95 × 10−5 F/cm2 with changes in the scan rate. These variations in C can be attributed to limited transport of the redox species at the interface of the GCE and the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate at the higher scan rate (Fig. 4C). The observed analytical performance of the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate towards Gln oxidation was validated using the calibration curves drawn over the Gln concentration range of 100–9000 µM (Fig. 4D). The CV-based LOD and LOQ values towards Gln detection were determined to be 360.4 and 1,100.3 µM, respectively. These values depicted a high sensitivity of the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate towards electrochemical sensing of Gln.

PL sensing of Gln was also performed using the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate. The major objective of this study was to provide a comparative and critical analysis of Gln quantification relative to CV analysis. Fig. 5 A shows the variation in PL intensity of the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate over varying concentrations of Gln (100–1,000 µM). The PL intensity of the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate (black curve) was similar to the pristine GQDs (red curve), as shown in Fig. 5A. We hereby propose a simple mechanism to understand the PL quenching of the Gln induced quenching of GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate. The hydrolysis of Gln by the conjugates generates NH3 and glutamate according to the biochemical reaction as shown below:

The NH3 molecule produced as a result of Gln hydrolysis act as an electron acceptor molecule owing to its high electron affinity. Consequently, it inhibits the radiative electron-hole recombination in the photoexcited GQDs. Many previous studies have reported that electrophiles such as O2 [47], SO3 − ions [48], and carbon nanotubes [49] readily reduce the PL intensity of GQDs to support our hypothesis. Experimentally, a hierarchical decrease in PL intensity of the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate was observed with increasing Gln concentration (Fig. 5A) to verify our hypothesis. The enzymatic response observed in the PL study is further associated with non-radiative recombination of charge carriers upon binding of Gln to the glutaminase enzyme present on the surface of the GQDs. The Ksv for Gln-induced quenching of the bioconjugate was found to be 2.2 × 103 M−1, which quantitatively represents the sensing characteristics of the bioconjugate. The observed analytical performance of the PL sensing method was validated using the calibration curve as shown in Fig. 5B. The observed linear range for Gln quantification was determined to be 200–9000 µM (Fig. 5B). The LOD and LOQ values for Gln quantification using the PL approach were determined to be 9.42 and 28.57 µM, respectively. On a comparative note, the PL-based detectability (e.g., in terms of LOD and LOQ values) for Gln was ~40 times lower than those of the CV analysis, supporting the superior sensitivity of the PL method. The selectivity of our sensor was further compared with two-fold concentration (2000 µM) of relevant interfering biochemicals (e.g., glutamate, aspartate (Asp) and asparagine (Asn). The PL intensity of GQDs-Glutaminase conjugate showed relatively very low quenching response to the other interfering compounds relative to Gln (Fig. S7). These observations suggested the selective PL response of our sensor to the Gln molecule.

Finally, the sensing performance of our GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate was compared with those reported previously (Table 2 ). Accordingly, Gln sensing with nanomaterials has been only sparsely described in the literature, supporting the importance of our studies. To offer better insights into the reliability of our biosensing approaches (relative to other options), the results summarized in Table 2 were evaluated further. The majority of the reported studies have utilized GQDs or their composites with glutaminase (Table 2). The minimum reported LOD value of 10 μM was based upon amperometric analysis of Gln using glutamine immobilized on a glass substrate (Table 2) [50]. Nonetheless, optical methods (e.g., fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–visible spectroscopy, and optical interferometry) have been more frequently used for Gln sensing relative to electrochemical sensing methods, as shown in Table 2. Such a preference for optical methods may be ascribed to their relatively low cost and facile operating conditions compared to electrochemical sensing methods. Moreover, the fluorescence spectroscopy approach yielded LOD values comparable to the electrochemical method. In fact, the LOD recorded for the PL method in our study was substantially lower than the previously reported studies for Gln sensing, demonstrating the high sensitivity of this sensing method. This comparison thus confirms the superiority of fluorescence-based methods over electrochemical methods for sensing applications. To further expand our study, our future endeavours should be directed toward the development of a nanocomposite of GQDs to enhance their sensitivity towards Gln detection.

The inherent PL characteristics of GQDs and the catalytic action of glutaminase makes the GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate an attractive biosensor for sensing Gln in an aqueous medium. Accordingly, this research described a precise and facile method for Gln detection based upon L-glutaminase-conjugated GQDs. The GQDs-glutaminase bioconjugate was tested for its Gln sensing abilities using CV- and PL-based approaches. The PL-based Gln detection method had lower LOD and LOQ values, highlighting the high sensitivity of this approach compared to the CV-based method.

ce:section.ce:para: Manpreet Kaur performed the tasks of investigation, data curation, and writing the original draft). Pooja Devi and Deepak Kukkar performed the duties of investigation, conceptualization, writing (review and editing) methodology, data curation, formal analysis, and writing (Review and Editing), and supervision of the work. Preeti Kukkar, Anupma Thakur performed the duties of data curation, and partial experimental studies. Harsimran Kaur and Ki-Hyun Kim performed conceptualization, formal Analysis, writing (review and editing), and supervision. Kamalpreet Kaur was involved in the review and editing of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by SERB, Government of India, under the early career research program (No. YSS/2015/000212). KHK acknowledges support from the R&D Center for Green Patrol Technologies through the R&D for Global Top Environmental Technologies funded by the Ministry of the Environment (MOE Grant No: 2018001850001) and by a grant from the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT, & Future Planning (Grant No: 2016R1E1A1A01940995).

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
